speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- describing Mengele, he was always meticulous in his uniform, always wore white gloves. Spoke a sort of clipped German language, all very short command sentences. There was no conversation. I can't sort of describe-- I can describe his physiognomy. I can't describe him-- describe him as a person, because I never saw him as a person. He never did anything as a person. Because it was all this clipped [? SS-alize ?] that he spoke. Even with the doctors, he didn't come down to a personal level. One or two jokes, yes. Perhaps the thing that would characterize him best was that this Jugendheim was block number 30, opposite the Krankenbau. And very shortly after I get-- got into the Krankenbau, you know, we all filed out when Mengele inspected Krankenbau. And Mengele would go out to his staff car and drive off. And Freddy Hirsch comes out of his block and intercepts Mengele, salutes, stands to attention. And Freddy Hirsch, I told you he came from Aachen. He spoke German with a completely German accent. And Freddie could imitate this [? SS-alize. ?] And he's asking Mengele to open, to allocate, to arrange-- to allocate block number 28-- 28-- 28, yes. No. [INAUDIBLE] We were 30, 28. Opposite was odd numbers, 29. Block number 27, which was the next but last. Block number 29 was last in the row. 27 was next but last. He wanted block number 27. And his reason was that the camp had filled out with many more children. And it would be a good idea now to separate the boys and the girls and have a block for the girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he's requesting for block number 27 to be allocated as a second Jugendheim. And I was sort of standing as far away as that door, you know, 10 feet. And I was standing to attention, thinking I'd be sent on one of these errands that Mengele always thought up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the thing that struck me was that Freddy Hirsch and Mengele were exactly the same types. They had the same sort of complexion. Perhaps Freddy's nose was a little bit more pointed. Freddy wasn't particularly Jewish looking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the complexion like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Complexion-- dark. Frankish, sort of-- you know, the Frankish race of dark people. Not necessarily Jewish, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they both spoke-- you know, the culture was obviously exactly the same. Freddy came out, boots, you know, riding boots, britches, a blue sort of tightly fitting jacket, the uniform that the prisoners, the smart prisoners. And he had his thing, [GERMAN]. You see? So that was his job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the way he saluted and spoke, and the way that Mengele spoke to him, I thought to myself, well, they're two men from the same-- you know, there's not-- race means nothing. They're the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Mengele's uniform like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you-- sort of blue military style jacket. It wasn't a uniform as such. But the prisoners made themselves look as if they were in uniform. And they would wear blue. Otherwise they'd make themselves special clothes, which were these striped pajama clothes. And some of them were dressed quite smartly, because there were tailors in-- in Auschwitz as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, I meant to ask you what Mengele's uniform was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was an SS uniform-- riding boots, britches. I'll tell you later about uniforms which are made for standing up. So it's rather funny thing. Swarthy, dark-looking men. But it occurred to me that the two of them could have been brothers. They were exactly the same height. And Mengele gave his permission to do it. Now I'm thinking to myself, if they had caught Mengele like they caught Eichmann, and if I had to go in a witness box, and somebody would ask me, did he do this, and did he-- did you hear him sanction it, I'd have to say, yes, he did sanction it. And it would be a jolly good defense for him, you know, extenuating circumstances. Would be very hard for-- for a judge to consider. Because Mengele might have said in his defense that he saved whoever he could. And there would be witnesses who would say the same. I know a woman who has said that he saved her mother that way. And she was his female runner in the female camp. She lives here in London. I don't know her-- I mean, I don't know her personally, but I've spoken to her. And she says the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you mentioned earlier on that you went to get the hernia belts and the aspirin. What other duties did you have with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kinds. He would send me on an errand to ask in the [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [GERMAN]. There was a book which was the orders of the day. In the orders of the day, how many sick people are in this block? He could have asked the block altester, but he would send me to run to the front of the camp to come back. Things like that. He'd-- he'd send me on the most stupid errands. And he just liked to see me run. It gave him some sort of buzz. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were out, you-- you went outside the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, when I-- when I was given some sort of task to go outside the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What might you have gone outside the camp for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I once went to the female camp. And it was something to do with clothes and bandages. These sort of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never went to Auschwitz I. I-- the first thing I asked Harry Lowett, do you remember how you walk from Birkenau to Auschwitz? Because I only remember going once, and that was on the death march. He doesn't remember at all. Anyway. Was it-- was it-- did the thought ever occur to you that there might be a possibility to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we left a few days later, after-- a couple of days after the selection. There was a call through the camp. All those who were selected to leave into the Maennerlager should report to the gate. So we all reported. I thought I was told that we would be 100. But some other of the boys said we were 96. Perhaps some didn't report.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you allowed for Mengele [? for? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, only in the Familienlager, because Heller said to me, you're going to be in this Maennerlager. And when you get to the Maennerlager, find yourself a job as a Laeufer, go to all the departments, but don't go to KB. Keep out of Mengele's way. So I said, why? And he said, people tend to disappear. Now we were take-- we-- we were standing, like at appel, five abreast. And we were counted several times. And then our relatives were allowed to come and say goodbye to us-- you know, mothers and fathers, and grandparents and so on. And it was getting dark. And the Germans were very nervous at having so many people near the gate. And it was all very emotive as well, because the boys were being marched off, going to be marched off. And-- relatives who would stay behind would die. There was only my mother left, because Traute left as a woman on a working thing. She got to Bergen-Belsen, Leo's wife. Leo and Milush went to Schwarzheide with another-- the working men, so there was only my mother left. I keep remembering it like when you say goodbye to somebody at a railway station, you know, at the-- at the train. People get on a train, and they open the window, and they tell you goodbye. Then you say goodbye again. The train still doesn't go, you see. And this was like that. We were like half an hour standing there. And all I was afraid was that my mother would start crying, or I would start crying, or some of the other boys would start crying. Nobody cried. There was sort of a stillness.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what do you say to your mother? You know she's going to die. Do you talk about death? You can't. What do you say? You've got nothing to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally it occurred to me to say to my mother, as if, how will we get out of this? You know, making it seem as if we have a chance. And she replied in Yiddish, [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't ask her anymore, because I didn't believe in God then either. I don't know how anyone can. I don't know how my mother did. And so we just stood there. And then we marched off, and that's the last I saw her. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't remember that there was a look of despair on her face. There was certainly no hysteria. She was just stoic. And it took a long time. It took, like, an age. And I already wanted to march off. Because I couldn't bear standing there any more. I've tried to talk like this to the people-- you know, to the other Birkenau boys-- about this memory of standing there. And not one of them remembers it the way that I do. Not one of them. They've all shut it out. I don't know why I haven't shut it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I keep thinking about my mother's tragedy. Because she made this tremendous mistake. She went in 1935 to the Maccabiah in Palestine to see her sister. And my father sent her with a million crowns. You know what that was before the war? What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about 150,000 pounds in money. And she was to sign contracts to buy 36 parcels of land, which was the whole of Ben Yehuda Street on the eastern side. The western side was apparently built at the time. And she was to buy the 36 parcels of land from [? Mugrabi ?] along Ben Yehuda, all the way to the Yarkon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she went to visit her sister. And part of the deal was that one of the parcels of land would be bought for her sister and that her husband-- who, by the way, was leasing deck chairs on Tel Aviv beach and was very poor-- and that was going to be their livelihood. Because the investment in the eastern side of Ben Yehuda Street would be his livelihood. And they would own one of the parcels. My Aunt Perla, her younger sister, contracted tuberculosis of the nerves during the First World War. And Zeev, my uncle, married her and she was already sick. She bore three children. They moved to Italy because the warm weather would be better for them. But he was a Zionist. He was into-- he was a Revisionist. We were all socialists, you know. Maccabi Hatzair is kibbutz and socialist. But he was-- he was a personal friend of Jabotinsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this affect your mother's decision?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait. So they came to Palestine 1925. My mother was very worried about my Uncle Itzchak, who was a very wayward boy-- young man by this time. She wanted to marry him off. The three children inherited my great-grandfather's pub in [? Tarnowiec. ?] And my mother-- I told her she wasn't a stupid woman-- she organized that Perla gave my mother power of attorney. And my mother and-- arranged a match with Auntie Lea. She came from a family called Neustetl. Lea was a nice young woman, but she was poor. And Itzchak would have never married to her because she didn't have a dowry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother-- this just will explain how she thought-- my mother arranged as a wedding present and as a dowry for Lea, she gave Lea 2/3 of the house. And this was like designed. This wayward brother of theirs, Itzchak, Itzchak Engelberg, would marry because of the dowry and because the whole house would be theirs. But 2/3 of the house would be controlled by the wife, not by the brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, this is how my mother thought out this situation, that his waywardness would have to stop. Of course, the war came and he was a father like everybody else. And my cousins are wonderful people. And he was a wonderful man. They all survived in Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you missed [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: But the tragedy was that she had this tuberculosis of the nerve endings and she couldn't write. So letters from Palestine to my mother would be, 'Perla says,' and Uncle Zeev would write for my-- for his wife. And my mother wrote letters, you know, saying, yes, you know, we are now well-to-do, we'll pay you, and this is what we propose to do. What do you think of it? And Uncle Zeev wrote, Perla says you should come. Come.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally-- you know, I told you my mother had this abortion, probably 1934ish or so. '35 was the Maccabiah, and there were cheap tickets. So she went in '35.

speaker: interviewee
text: She arrives and my uncle says to her, I just got a telegram whilst you were already on the way that there is a new cure for tuberculosis of the nerve endings. And I have sent Perla to Switzerland to my brother, this address that I had throughout the war of the [INAUDIBLE]. I've just sent her to Switzerland for a cure. And my mother arrives at the flat where they lived. She sees there's no woman here. So she gets hold of an Arab woman and she starts cleaning, you know, getting organized. And she has two tickets for her and Perla to go to the Maccabiah. And Perla isn't here, so she takes Itzchak, my eldest cousin, the eldest-- Itzchak Chari the eldest son. And it's-- I don't know if you know Ramat Gan in Tel Aviv. There was a new stadium built for the Maccabiah. And the stadium is still there in Ramat Gan. And Itzchak now tells me, they arrive at the Maccabiah and Arab youths are throwing stones. So the Haganah comes. And in those days, in '35, they had sticks. They didn't-- you know, it wasn't like the army in those days. And they started beating up on these Arab kids. And Itzchak says, your mother gets off the bus shrieking away, folding her parasol, and she is hitting the Haganah youths with her parasol, shouting in them-- at them in Yiddish, [YIDDISH], you don't beat small children. You see, the Haganah was fighting Arab youths who were throwing stones, at [INAUDIBLE] buses. It was the Maccabiah. And my cousin Itzchak was a Revisionist, you know, in the Jabotinsky party, the right wing, you see. He says, I was so ashamed of this woman, you know, this crazy woman hitting the Haganah. So I was trying to drag her away. I said, look, you don't understand. We've got a war here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she didn't buy the land.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was just-- no, nothing to do with the land yet. He was just a lad like me, you know. Itzchak is a bit older than me. He's about 70 by now. He's 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: That affected her decision, though, to purchase.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. So Itzchak drags her into the stadium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just tell me briefly, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they start marching. And Itzchak is full of it, you know. It's the Maccabiah. And she says, you wouldn't talk to me like this if your mother was here. And Itzchak says to her, you stupid woman. My mother has been dead these three years. We're without her. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he says, my mother started crying whilst all this marching was going on. And he says, I suddenly felt that I've done something wrong. Because my aunt didn't, in fact, know Perla committed suicide in 1933. And suicide is such a terrible thing in Jewish religious usage.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't have TB and she didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: She had TB. But she was such a burden to her family that she committed suicide. She already infirm. She was in a wheelchair. And my cousins described this. So she committed suicide. And these three boys were already without their mother. And she was telling the eldest, if your mother was here-- so he-- he had his own hurt. And that affected her. And the fact that the Jews, the Haganah, was beating Arab children affected her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she saw all this sand in Tel Aviv, you know. Ben Yehuda was undeveloped on the eastern side. My uncle was still alive because I was in Israel in 1948. He says she said, all this sand, who wants to buy all this sand? I think we'll back now to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, she didn't buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was-- she didn't obey her father-- her-- her husband. And she didn't sign the contract. What's more, the one parcel that she was obligated to give to the [INAUDIBLE] family, because she took a third of the house to give to Lea, the other aunt, you see, as a dowry-- she gave them the money and she advised her in-law, Zeev Charif, buy yourself a [INAUDIBLE]. And Michael, my cousin, says, you would have been millionaires if your mother had done what your father said. But we would have been millionaires if we had just bought that one parcel. I mean, we made a lot of money because we bought a [INAUDIBLE]. But he bought a [INAUDIBLE] in the countryside. So this was an error. And I wonder-- I keep thinking, when I was saying goodbye to her, the tragedy sort of was at her feet, you know, that she had brought three sons into captivity. So her meddling or muddling, and so not obeying, so following her own mind. And she was just wrong. I mean, it's no fault of hers. But when I was 16, I was still bitter about the fault of hers, the-- the-- the tremendous error, you know, of judgment. But to me, this is a moment that I remember. Because I remember this tragic error that we were there but for the chance of my aunt committing suicide, and my uncle writing to her, come and buy, and denying to her that her sister was-- had committed suicide. It's-- I mean, it-- it tells like a film, you know, the whole-- the years leading up to the Shoah. And then the Shoah, I'm talking to her. And I know none of this, because all of this I had to figure out for myself as I grew up.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I visited Israel in 1948, I went on this course for youth leaders. And I visited my uncle and I spoke in detail. I visited my cousin Itzchak. And we talked long hours--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and we got on, and we became family again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think now we'll-- we'll go back to what happened after you were parted from your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got to the Maennerlager. And the Germans did things by strange logic. They put us into a Straffenblock. Inside the compound of the Maennerlager, which was D, like I told you, there was a compound of blocks 11-- no, 9, 11, and 13. 9 and 11 was where the Sonderkommando lived. And 13 was the Straffenblock. Prisoners-- it was like a jail inside a jail. And-- and they put these boys, these 96 or 100 boys-- you know, the Birkenau boys-- into this Straffenblock. I-- as soon as I heard that it's a Straffenblock and I saw that this was a Straffen transport, I thought to myself, I've got to get out of here. And the washing-up facilities for these three blocks were in the middle, in the Sonderkommando block.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next morning-- I mean, I concluded straightaway, I've got to get out of here. We're washing, and there's a man washing next to me. And I said, can you take me out of this Straffenblock. So he says, what's wrong? So I said, I just don't feel well about it. I think we're in the wrong place. So he said, OK. I'll see to it. Let's stop here.
